item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If one died, their consciousness would survive in the other's mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my favorites. Though I was left with the question of why, knowing that they are telepathically linked, do they keep them separated since it seems that both of them are, at the very least, content with their situation. (Well, that and the nagging question I always have about linked pairs…if one dies, does the other?)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh never mind, found the answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if one of her arms were cut off? Would it grow back or would something else happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could really tell this was a hands-on project. I think the amount of appendages got out of hand… but, then, that's out of my hands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess she raised her arms and pointed with her chin or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it. The description of them were disturbing, in a good SCP-like way. Although I would suggest changing the class from Safe to Euclid as we are talking about an old woman who can't eat or get dressed (and other stuff). If you think about the Box Theory, leaving her alone would result in her death for the lack of care thus most humanoid SCPs belong to Euclid object class.
Also I was a bit confused when she was talking about her injuries, like she was pointing at them "This I got when….that when…". It sounds weird cause she can't truly point at anything if her forearms are joined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this one. Favorite bit has to be
Dr. Selman: How would you describe your captivity in the Circus Of The Disquieting?

SCP-1884-B: (SCP-1884-B raises the middle finger of every hand not being used to support its weight)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made me feel for a bunch of hands. That's brilliant. Upvoted

item.title: Heartwarming
item.content:encoded:
It is not common that life in SCP containment is portrayed as an improvement over life in the cruel outside world.

I love it.

item.title: No Such Group Exists?
item.content:encoded: I was just wondering if this was the first mention of Herman Fuller's Circus. The researcher says no such group exists, but the Circus is a recognized GOI and according to it's hub page there are 11 skips linked to it.

Another hundred! Give 1884 a big show of hands!

fufufufufufu, puns

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oddly touching

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1884-B: (SCP-1884-B raises the middle finger of every hand not being used to support its weight)

Aaaand upvoted lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every herman fuller scp gets me more and more interested in this storyline.

item.title: Re: Nice touches...
item.content:encoded: Read SCP-1931. Amelia the Tiger in that Skip is the same one Luana got her paw from.

item.title: Nice touches...
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for the nice touches in the description and interviews.

How did Luana get a tiger's paw?

  • at some point they escaped into a wilderness and had to fight a predator
  • one of the circus cats was mistreated and escaped, attacking them (most likely)
  • Luana was forced to fight a tiger for a show audience (!)

It's a great example of a single detail only saying one thing and suggesting a whole lot. I also got that Luana likes books with sensitive, intelligent women characters trapped by social circumstances who struggle with the desire to be rescued. Lots of good little things all around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just getting to this one, love it to death.

I'm still have a little trouble really picturing Luana; how densely are her hands packed on her central mass? Does she look like a solid coat of hands on the outside? How flexible is she? Like almost an amorphous hand-blob or more of a rigid hand-sea-urchin?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is somehow both heartbreaking and heartwarming all at the same time. I love how it kind of hints at the idea that life as a SCiP might not be as bad as life on the outside could be. Or at least that's how I read it. And it's really neat to see the occasional sentient set of entities that are so unfailingly polite to their assigned researchers and the like. I upvoted this a lot. Or would have if you could give more than one. But take my +1. I would like you to have it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Luana is so… endearingly polite. "MAY I HAVE BRAILLE BOOKS PLEASE", emphasis added. I kinda want to give the bizarre hand-thing a hug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HAND THING

Aren't you just adorable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i want to give this two upvotes… that doesn't happen very often.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really really well thought out, and it all comes through in the article itself.

By the way, I absolutely fell in love with Luana when she described herself as "HAND THING."

+ Bloop
Luana is pleased.

You guys are awesome. <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A great SCP. I love when -B asks for the braille books, definitely sold me on her humanity.

I also like reading about the various circus related SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt a connection with this, well written and compassionate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awww, the hand thing is kind of adorable. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It sounds like from time to time there was someone in the audience the circus decided to, "get rid of" for unexplained reasons, though if they are doing shady business I can only imagine.

Also, upvote, if only for the mass of hands giving the finger with all available hands. Can we get a pic of that somewhere?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Selman: Who else did they hurt?

SCP-1884-B: OTHER ACTS. AND SOMETIMES PEANUT GALLERY.

Wait, the circus would sometimes hurt their paying customers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it, then loved it based on the twin interviews. Really, author, major kudos for treating and understanding your characters as people, even the "HAND THING".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's all-caps because it's braille, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I see. Thanks for elaborating on that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! As Rezarta said later in her story, the man with the upside-down face works at the circus. When you have a guy like that around, it's hard not to hear about it at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love your creativity and imagination, +1! My only problem with the whole thing was when the man with the upside-down face came to take Rezarta and Luana away. I mean, how did Rezarta know the man had an upside-down face when she is blind?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's weird, mysterious, and very fun to read. Definitely got my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I heartily approve of this weird and interesting SCP. Glad this turned out to be a sort of happy ending for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Luana strikes me as the best possible way to turn The Luggage into an scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it weird that I imagined an accent for the hands, but not the person?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great article, although I found myself becoming far more interested in the circus than the skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy crap I love how much character you can pack into 932 hands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I finally found a less awkward way to describe A's deformities, so that should help a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hard to imagine what either of them would actually look like. Oh well, this is still a really good one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I made an error in my English I would be whipped. My English improved very quickly.

All these scars are from the Circus. I received this one when I misused the word 'competent'. This one, I confused a Roman nose with an Aquiline nose.

Sounds fine to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed the same thing a while after I posted this. This one's been in the pipes for a couple of months now, though. The actual writing process for this started right after I published 1808. So yeah, odd timing coincidence. Though it's possible reading 1972 gave my brain the subconscious push to get this one finished and out the door.

Also-this isn't really important-but I like to think that the offbeat one has more of a mind than the human one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted mainly for sassy HAND THING.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two weird entities, the first is a mostly humanoid woman, the other is something really offbeat. They have a past together, and are in fact only written up as one anomaly because of how they interact. After describing them, the article has an interview with each of them. The humanoid describes their circumstances in a reasonable and coherent manner without much ado. The offbeat one has a strange manner of speaking (which is also rendered in all-caps) and has less of a human mind than the woman. The article contains no content after these interviews.

Plus they were posted within a day of each other (or very close to it). So yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why? Do tell, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should probably also mention that I got serious flashbacks to SCP-1972 from this for some reason. I know goddamn well why I got flashbacks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with the others. I like that that B is so disarming yet it doesn't possess a personality that is alien or naturally hostile to people; it may just be a very "unique" twin sister who's actions are born of desperation and an abused life. Humans are the greatest monsters, yadda yadda. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was intentional. Rezarta and Luana were treated differently by the Circus, and their differing experiences affected their personalities. As the mouthpiece and show personality, Rezarta was conditioned to speak and act in a very specific way. Luana had much more freedom since people didn't expect as much from an apparent monster. Thus, Rezarta grew up to be very submissive and reserved whereas Luana is proactive and candid. You see this in their respective interviews, particularly in how they end: Rezarta focuses on buttering up the people holding her captive even though she's not entirely pleased with the situation, but Luana is outspoken and makes an effort to improve her quality of life.

Basically, I thought it would be interesting to have a human/monster duo where the monster seemed more human than the human did. One's outward human appearance led to her losing some of her internal humanity. One's monstrous appearance afforded her much more leeway in how she developed. And yet, in the end, one gets to stay in a residential chamber with an attendant while the other ends up alone in a cage, all because of their outward appearances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get a very, very strange Calvin & Hobbes vibe from this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man I like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not sure why, but I find it hearwarming, and that's so good. Upvoted.

By the way, I found -B's personality more itneresting than -A's

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean size-wise.

Oh, and I hope to hell the upside-down-faced man shows up as a skip or something. Because that shit is tremendously scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I struggled with that line a lot. Your suggestion sounds pretty good, but it could still be easily misread. I'll sit on it for a while and see if I can come up with the perfect phrasing.

Mr. Google says that the average weight for a human hand is around 400g. Multiply that by 932 and you have 373kg (822lbs). Add in the weight for the central mass of flesh and you have one really, really big hand thing, which is what I imagined in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ohh!

Hmm. I'm not sure if there's a better way to phrase that or not. Let's take a look at the line:

SCP-1884-A's elbows lead to a shared forearm with no additional hands or connective joints of any sort.

I think focusing on the elbows is what makes it confusing. Admittedly, it could go either way. Something more along the lines of "SCP-1884-A's forearms connect at the midpoint and bear no additional hands or connective joints of any sort." There's probably another word you could fit in there to make it absolutely clear (I'm thinking something along the lines of 'linearly'), but anatomy jargon is not my strong suit.

Oh, and before I forget: how big is Luana?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! Yup, the other articles I have planned will be related to the same circus mentioned.

As for her arms, that's not quite what I had in mind when I wrote this. Take a look at this image and imagine that there are no hands beneath the hand warmer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really freaky, wonderful stuff. If there's going to be more related to this nonexistant circus, I anticipate it with relish.

One question, just so I'm clear what I'm seeing here: her arms are essentially connected at the elbow, from which extends a single forearm with no hand? (To the wrist, I assume.) Basically a Y with the stem pointed away from her body?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely well-served by the SCP format, I think. The first interview risks being overly expository, but, of course, the entire point of an interview is exposition, and the character herself discusses her ability/need to create and control a narrative (which becomes thematically significant when you consider the actual SCP, which is all about agency). Also, it plays a bit with the usual meta viewer stuff inherent to stories about the circus: We're watching the audience watch the "freak", but now it's also mediated both by the freak-as-narrator and, of course, by the Foundation and its place in the SCP catalog. This makes me want to tickle this article's tummy, as if it were an adorable kitten.

Upvoted, obviously. Very nice work, dude.
